message.content:
1. **The “Public Shame Sprint” (30–90 minutes a day)**  
   Use mild social pressure on purpose.  
   - Pick one important task the night before. Break it into a 30–90 minute *visible* work block.  
   - Tell a specific person: “From 9:30–10:15 I’m working on X. I’ll send you a screenshot/photo of what I got done at 10:15. If I don’t, I owe you $20 / I’ll do [unpleasant chore].”  
   - Set a timer, block all distractions (phone in another room, email closed), and work until the timer goes off. Then send proof, even if you barely did anything.  
   Why it works: You’re weaponizing your fear of looking like a flake. No motivation required; just not wanting to lose money or face.

2. **The “Embarrassingly Small Minimum” Rule**  
   Your new rule: *You are allowed to half-ass anything, but you are not allowed to zero-ass it.*  
   - For every task you’re dodging, define a ridiculous minimum that takes 2–5 minutes and still counts as “did it today”:  
     - Write 1 sentence of the report.  
     - Reply to 1 email that’s been rotting.  
     - Open the document and add 3 bullet points.  
   - Put these minimums on your calendar as actual events. When the time comes, do the bare minimum and then you’re allowed to quit.  
   - If you overshoot and keep going, fine. But the only thing that “fails” the day is doing zero.  
   Why it works: You’re lowering the bar so hard that your usual excuses sound pathetic even to you. It gets you past the start line, which is where you actually choke.

3. **Deliberate Self-Sabotage Audit (End-of-day, 10 minutes)**  
   Instead of pretending you’ll “do better tomorrow,” audit how you actually screwed yourself today. No journaling fluff, just a brutal list.  
   - At the end of each workday, set a 10-minute timer.  
   - Write 3 columns:  
     1. “What I avoided” – name 3 tasks you dodged.  
     2. “How I dodged it” – be specific (YouTube, pretending to ‘research,’ cleaning, doomscrolling).  
     3. “How I made it worse” – concrete impact (now I need to rush, missed a chance to ask for help, weekend is toast).  
   - Then write one single sentence: “Tomorrow I will do [X] for [Y minutes] *before* I’m allowed to do [habitual distraction].” Example: “I will work on the slide deck for 25 minutes before I open YouTube.”  
   - Put that block on your calendar or set an alarm for it.  
   Why it works: You’re forcing yourself to stare at the cost of your behavior daily, then tie work to the thing you usually run to. No “insight,” just pattern → consequence → tiny rule.

Use all three for one week. Don’t wait to “feel ready”; just schedule them right now and let your future self curse you when the alarms go off. That version of you will still get more done than your usual approach.
